frames.caption: Compute MaxDepth(root): depth(node) = 1 + max(depth(left), depth(right)), with the null child returning 0.
frames.note: This is the universal tree-DP shape. The recursion returns ONE number describing the subtree rooted at each node — its depth — so the parent can combine the two children's answers. We descend left, descend right, then combine on the way back up.

frames.caption: Visit node 5. It is not null, so recurse into its LEFT child (3) first.
frames.note: This is depth-first search: before we can answer for a node, we must know how deep each of its subtrees goes, so we dive into the LEFT child first, then the right. The call stack holds the chain of ancestors we still owe an answer to — its depth is the height of the tree, which is the O(h) space cost.

frames.caption: Visit node 3. It is not null, so recurse into its LEFT child (1) first.
frames.note: This is depth-first search: before we can answer for a node, we must know how deep each of its subtrees goes, so we dive into the LEFT child first, then the right. The call stack holds the chain of ancestors we still owe an answer to — its depth is the height of the tree, which is the O(h) space cost.

frames.caption: Visit node 1. It is not null, so recurse into its LEFT child (null) first.
frames.note: This is depth-first search: before we can answer for a node, we must know how deep each of its subtrees goes, so we dive into the LEFT child first, then the right. The call stack holds the chain of ancestors we still owe an answer to — its depth is the height of the tree, which is the O(h) space cost.

frames.caption: Hit a null child → return 0. An empty subtree contributes depth 0.
frames.note: The base case is the null child. An empty subtree has depth 0 — the identity we build on. Reaching null is also what stops the recursion from running forever; every leaf bottoms out into two null calls that each return 0.

frames.caption: Left subtree of 1 returned depth 0. Now recurse into its RIGHT child (null).
frames.note: Post-order means we finish the entire left subtree before touching the right. We stash its returned depth (left) and repeat the same descent on the right — same problem, smaller input. Only once BOTH are back can this node answer.

frames.caption: Hit a null child → return 0. An empty subtree contributes depth 0.
frames.note: The base case is the null child. An empty subtree has depth 0 — the identity we build on. Reaching null is also what stops the recursion from running forever; every leaf bottoms out into two null calls that each return 0.

frames.caption: Both children of 1 are back (left=0, right=0) → return 1 + max(0, 0) = 1.
frames.note: Post-order: we only combine AFTER both children have returned. depth(node) = 1 + max(left, right) — the "+1" counts this node itself, and max picks the deeper of the two subtrees because the longest path must go down one side. The value flows UP to the parent, which will add its own +1.

frames.caption: Left subtree of 3 returned depth 1. Now recurse into its RIGHT child (4).
frames.note: Post-order means we finish the entire left subtree before touching the right. We stash its returned depth (left) and repeat the same descent on the right — same problem, smaller input. Only once BOTH are back can this node answer.

frames.caption: Visit node 4. It is not null, so recurse into its LEFT child (null) first.

The call stack holds the chain of ancestors we still owe an answer to — its depth is the height of the tree, which is the O(h) space cost.

frames.caption: Both children of 5 are back (left=2, right=2) → return 1 + max(2, 2) = 3.
frames.note: Post-order: we only combine AFTER both children have returned. depth(node) = 1 + max(left, right) — the "+1" counts this node itself, and max picks the deeper of the two subtrees because the longest path must go down one side. The value flows UP to the parent, which will add its own +1.

frames.caption: MaxDepth(root) = 3. The longest root-to-leaf path has 3 nodes: 5 -> 3 -> 1.
frames.note: Each node is visited exactly once and does O(1) combining work, so the whole traversal is O(n) time. The answer at the root, 3, is the number of nodes on the longest root-to-leaf path (5 -> 3 -> 1). That is LeetCode's "maximum depth": tree height in edges, plus one.
